name: Aligne
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a geometric sans; the contrast system departs from both translation and expansion models, deriving relationships from letterform shapes rather than calligraphic stroke logic.
summary: A geometric sans by Troy Leinster that derives its contrast from the shapes of letterforms rather than from calligraphic pen models.
description: Ten weights run from a near-zero Hairline to an extreme-contrast Ultra, with the heaviest cuts distributing thin strokes through the interior so letters appear assembled from discrete parts. The contrast logic is neither translation nor expansion but something the designer calls shape-relational, making it unusual among display geometric sans families. Available as a variable font with stylistic alternates including a simplified 'a' and 'g', stovepipe 't', and capital punctuation, it is positioned for fashion, editorial, packaging and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Leinster Type describe Aligne as a geometric sans that takes a different approach to construction and contrast, departing from both broad-nib and pointed-nib calligraphic models. Instead, contrast is derived from relationships between letterform shapes, so that in heavier weights thin strokes are distributed throughout the exterior forms, giving the appearance of letters assembled on the page. The family spans ten weights from Hairline to Ultra and is available as a variable font with Latin Extended language support.

name: Bite
classification_notes: Octagonal, heavy letterforms derived from industrial signage and military stencil traditions place this firmly in display; the geometric construction and stencil variants distinguish it from standard grotesque or gothic categories.
summary: An octagonal display face by Troy Leinster built for heavy-industry and action-sports branding, offered in solid and stencil cuts.
description: Four styles cover Upright, Oblique, Stencil and Stencil Oblique, with the solid and stencil variants designed to work together as a flexible identity system. The letterforms draw on industrial signage and military stencils, with a broad surface area suited to scale and extreme environments. OpenType features include stylistic alternates, discretionary ligatures, capital punctuation and arrows.
foundry_blurb: Leinster Type describe Bite as built for high-impact brands, taking cues from industrial signage, military stencils and heavy machinery. They position it for branding heavy equipment, fast cars, outdoor gear, security, athletic apparel, off-road biking and surfing culture, noting that the octagonal letterforms hold their weight at scale across extreme environments and statement identities.

name: Brisbane
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a humanist sans serif, with open forms, generous x-height and low contrast characteristic of the humanist tradition, suited to text and wayfinding applications.
summary: An economically proportioned humanist sans by Troy Leinster, built for text legibility and wayfinding, with a strong OpenType feature set including arrows and location markers.
description: Ten styles span five weights from Light to Bold with companion italics, and a variable font version allows fine-grained weight adjustment. Open forms and a generous x-height aid legibility at small sizes and at distance, making it appropriate for signage, software interfaces and editorial text alike. The OpenType feature set goes beyond the usual alternates to include circled figures, circled arrows, chevrons and location markers, giving it practical utility for civic and wayfinding contexts.
foundry_blurb: Leinster Type describe Brisbane as a humanist sans designed for legibility, with open forms, a generous x-height and low contrast suited to small text and distant signage. The ten-style family runs from Light to Bold with italics, is available as a variable font, and includes OpenType features such as stylistic alternates for character recognition, oldstyle figures, circled figures, arrows and location markers. It is positioned for editorial text, software interfaces, signage, and identity work for cultural, health and community organisations.

name: Gardein
classification_notes: Built on a Roman foundation with concave stems, flared terminals and Arts and Crafts-influenced quirks; the foundry explicitly classes it as a humanist serif optimised for display and headline use.
summary: A humanist serif with concave stems and flared terminals, drawn for organic branding, packaging and headline work.
description: Designed by Christopher Caldwell and Troy Leinster, it takes inspiration from the book cover work of Arts and Crafts designer Sarah W. Whitman, translating that influence into a five-weight family with extra weight concentrated at the x-height and baseline. The result is a type with distinct surface texture suited to eco-friendly brands, book covers, wayfinding and environmental graphics. Available as a variable font across the full Light-to-Extra Bold range.
foundry_blurb: Leinster Type describe Gardein as a humanist serif with unconventional quirks, built on a Roman foundation with concave stems adorned with flared terminals and extra weight at the x-height and baseline. Inspired by the book cover design of American Arts and Crafts-era artist Sarah W. Whitman, the family spans five weights and is also offered as a variable font, aimed at headlines, short texts and eco-friendly branding.

name: Multiflex
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as geometric at its core but humanist in flavour, with notch-like connections and open forms that soften the underlying geometric structure. This places it squarely in the geometric humanist sans category.
summary: A geometric sans with humanist softening across four widths and nine weights, designed to cover everything from caption text to display headlines within a single family.
description: Thirty-six styles span four widths, from Compact to Wide, and nine weights from Thin to Black, with variable font support throughout. Notch-like connections at joins and a generous x-height pull the geometry back from rigidity, keeping the face readable at small sizes without losing its machine-cut quality. Stylistic alternates include single- and double-storey options for 'a' and 'g', adding some flexibility for branding contexts.
foundry_blurb: Leinster Type describe Multiflex as geometric at its core but humanist in flavour, with multiple weights and flexible widths allowing it to work across tight editorial columns and expansive headlines without falling out of character. Notch-like connections soften the geometry, while a generous x-height and open forms maintain readability at caption sizes. The family spans 36 styles across four widths and nine weights, and is available as a variable font with extended Latin and Vietnamese language support.

name: Retain
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a contemporary grotesque for everyday use, with upright-only weights, alternate letterforms and optical tuning across caption to headline sizes typical of the genre.
summary: A nine-weight grotesque sans with a variable axis and over ten stylistic sets, designed for use across interfaces, editorial work and identity systems.
description: Nine upright weights run from Hairline to Extra Bold, with a variable font unlocking additional steps between them. More than ten stylistic sets allow alternate letter shapes, a choice of round or square dots and subtle character variants, so the family can shift tone without changing its underlying structure. Extras include discretionary ligatures, case-sensitive punctuation, multiple figure sets, arrows, symbols and coverage for over 390 languages including Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: Leinster Type describe Retain as a contemporary grotesque for everyday use that offers multiple voices within a single family. The ten-plus stylistic sets, choice of round or square dots and alternate forms give it range while maintaining a consistent look and feel, and the family is optically tuned from caption to headline sizes with extensive OpenType features and support for over 390 languages.

name: Sedgwick
classification_notes: An upright, unconnected script drawing on civilité types of the 15th and 16th centuries and a Dutch metal type revival; mannered letterforms with distinct stroke contrast place it firmly in the display script tradition.
summary: An upright, unconnected display script by Troy Leinster, drawing on Dutch metal type and civilité forms for fashion branding and short poetic settings.
description: Sedgwick takes its structural cues from Lettergieterij Amsterdam's 1909 Nieuw Javaansch No.1 and the civilité types of the 15th and 16th centuries, arriving as a single upright style with mannered, unconnected letterforms. The lowercase drives a distinctive rhythmic texture, while the uppercase, figures and punctuation introduce unexpected forms suited to word-marks and headlines. OpenType features include stylistic alternates, contextual alternates, ligatures and capital punctuation, offering meaningful compositional control within a single weight.
foundry_blurb: Leinster Type describe Sedgwick as a mannered, upright, unconnected script with a touch of attitude, intended for short poetic texts and exquisite word-marks. Forms were inspired by the structure and stroke of Nieuw Javaansch No.1, Troy Leinster's own unreleased 2012 revival of the 1909 Lettergieterij Amsterdam typeface, and the civilité types of the 15th and 16th centuries. The typeface is named after Edie Sedgwick, Warhol's muse, and is positioned for fashion and luxury branding, headlines, book covers, packaging and retail graphics.
